title: Pork chops with cider and apples
subtitle: Pan-fried pork with a cider, cream, and apple sauce
excerpt: Thick pork chops browned in butter, finished in a pan sauce of dry cider, sliced apples, and a little cream. A British autumn dish with a French accent, straightforward and very good.
sourceNotes: Pork with cider and apples is a classic pairing in the cooking of the West Country, Normandy, and Brittany. In British cooking it appears in Jane Grigson's English Food (1974) and across twentieth-century British regional cookery as the standard way to cook pork with the flavours of the apple-growing counties.

content.text: Pork and apple is one of the most natural combinations in British cooking, and cider is the most direct expression of it. Use a dry cider rather than a sweet one: the sugar in sweet cider makes the sauce cloying. The apples should be a firm variety that holds shape during cooking. Cox or Braeburn are reliable; Bramley will dissolve into the sauce, which is not wrong but changes the character of the dish.
Bone-in pork chops, cut thick, give better flavour than boneless. The bone acts as an insulator and keeps the meat moist. Rest the chops for 5 minutes before serving.

items.prepNote: bone-in, about 250 g each, 2.5 cm thick
Cox or Braeburn, peeled, cored, sliced

content.text: Season {{pork-chop}} pork chops generously on both sides. Heat a frying pan over medium-high heat until hot. Add half the butter. When foaming, lay in the chops and cook for 4 minutes without moving. Turn and cook for 3 to 4 minutes more. The chops should be golden brown on both sides and cooked through — the juices run clear when the thickest part is pierced. Remove and rest on a warm plate.

In the same pan, add the remaining butter over medium heat. Cook {{shallot}} finely sliced shallots for 3 minutes until soft. Add {{apple}} apple slices and cook for 3 minutes until just beginning to colour. Pour in {{dry-cider}} of dry cider and let it bubble for 3 minutes until reduced by half. Stir in {{double-cream}} of double cream and the thyme. If using mustard, stir it in now. Cook for 2 minutes until the sauce coats the back of a spoon. Taste and season.
Return the chops to the pan and spoon the sauce over them. Serve immediately with mashed or baked potato and greens.

items.cause: Overcooked, or the chops were too thin
items.fix: Use chops at least 2.5 cm thick. Modern pork is very lean and dries out quickly. A thermometer reading of 65°C at the thickest point is correct — it is safe to eat slightly pink.

items.cause: Sweet cider was used, or apples were very sweet
items.fix: Use a dry cider only. Add a small squeeze of lemon juice to balance the sweetness.

items.symptom: Apples have dissolved into the sauce
items.cause: Too long in the pan, or a cooking apple was used
items.fix: Eating apples hold shape better. Cook them briefly and remove to the serving plate before reducing the sauce.

items.cause: Insufficient reduction or cream added too early
items.fix: Reduce the cider by at least half before adding the cream. The cream will thicken the sauce quickly once the liquid base is concentrated.

content.text: Pork with cider and apples is the signature dish of the English West Country, where apple orchards and pig-keeping have coexisted for centuries. Jane Grigson wrote about the combination in English Food (1974) and in her Fruit Book (1982), placing it firmly in the tradition of Somerset and Devon cooking. The same combination exists across the Channel in Normandy and Brittany, where pork, cider, and crème fraîche appear together in dishes like côtes de porc Vallée d'Auge. The British version uses double cream rather than crème fraîche but the flavour logic is identical.
